title: Neural Control: Adjoint Learning Through Equilibrium Constraints
html_content:
Neural Control: Adjoint Learning Through Equilibrium Constraints
Dezhong Tong
Jiawen Wang
Hengyi Zhou
Yinglong Shen
Xiaonan Huang
M. Khalid Jawed
Abstract
Many physical AI tasks are governed by implicit equilibrium: an agent actuates a subset of degrees of freedom (boundary DoFs), while the remaining free DoFs settle by minimizing a total potential energy. Even seemingly basic tasks such as bending a deformable linear object (DLO) to a target shape can exhibit strongly nonlinear behavior due to multi-stability: the same boundary conditions may yield multiple equilibrium shapes depending on the actuation trajectory. However, learning and control in such systems is brittle because the actuation-to-configuration map is defined only implicitly, and naive backpropagation through iterative equilibrium solvers is memory- and compute-intensive. We propose Neural Control, a boundary-control framework that computes trajectory-dependent, memory-efficient proxy gradients by differentiating equilibrium conditions via an adjoint formulation, avoiding unrolling of solver iterations. To improve robustness over long horizons, we integrate these sensitivities into a receding-horizon MPC scheme that repeatedly re-anchors optimization to realized equilibria and mitigates basin-switching in multi-stable regimes. We evaluate Neural Control in simulation and on physical robots manipulating DLOs, and show improved performance over gradient-free baselines such as SPSA and CEM.
Machine Learning, ICML
1
Introduction
Many physical systems encountered in robotics, graphics, and simulation are governed by an energy functional, with system states defined implicitly as solutions of energy minimization problems under boundary and loading conditions. In the absence of non-conservative forces, controlling such systems amounts to shaping the energy landscape вҖ“ by adjusting boundary conditions or parameters вҖ“ so that the resulting equilibrium achieves a desired objective. This setting poses a fundamental challenge: equilibria are high-dimensional and often multi-stable, with small changes in boundary actuation inducing discontinuous transitions between solution branches.
These difficulties are especially pronounced in robotic manipulation of deformable structures (Figure
1
A), such as deformable linear objects (DLOs)
(Mora
et al.
,
2021
; Li
et al.
,
2023
; Tong
et al.
,
2024
)
, where large, geometrically nonlinear deformations and hysteresis lead to multi-stable equilibria and path-dependent behavior
(Qiao
et al.
,
2020
; Liang
et al.
,
2019
; Huang
et al.
,
2025
; Bretl and McCarthy,
2014
; Tong
et al.
,
2021
)
. Figure
1
B illustrates this phenomenon, showing that a DLO can admit multiple stable equilibrium shapes under identical boundary conditions. A fundamental challenge is that state updates are defined only implicitly as the solution of equilibrium problems вҖ“ such as energy minimization вҖ“ over high-dimensional discretizations of continua
(Yin
et al.
,
2021
; Moll and Kavraki,
2006
; Bergou
et al.
,
2008
)
. As a result, this equilibrium map can be history-dependent due to basin-of-attraction effects, breaking standard Markov assumptions and making long-horizon planning and learning brittle
(Hausknecht and Stone,
2015
; Suh
et al.
,
2022
)
.
Differentiable simulation offers gradients for optimization
(Qiao
et al.
,
2020
; Mora
et al.
,
2021
; Suh
et al.
,
2022
)
, but multistable control requires branch-consistent, trajectory-dependent sensitivities that follow the realized branch selected by the solver
(Georgiev
et al.
,
2024
; Gao
et al.
,
2024
)
. Markovian transition models are inherently single-valued; in multi-stable systems, identical states and actions can converge to different equilibria, causing learned dynamics to average over branches unless latent modes are modeled explicitly
(Bishop,
1994
; Hafner
et al.
,
2019b
,
a
)
. Moreover, when each step requires an implicit equilibrium solve, backpropagating through solver iterations over long horizons is memory-intensive and numerically fragile near instabilities
(Gruslys
et al.
,
2016
; Amos and Kolter,
2017
; Bai
et al.
,
2019
)
.
This motivates a method that computes trajectory-dependent sensitivities through sequences of implicit equilibrium solves without unrolling inner iterations, while remaining robust to branch selection over long horizons.
Inspired by adjoint sensitivity methods in continuous-depth learning
(Chen
et al.
,
2018
)
,
we introduce an adjoint-based framework that computes memory-efficient, trajectory-dependent proxy gradients by differentiating equilibrium conditions, while keeping the forward rollout on converged equilibria along the realized branch.
To improve robustness in multi-stable regimes, we embed these sensitivities into a receding-horizon model predictive control (MPC) loop
(Chua
et al.
,
2018
)
that repeatedly replans from realized configurations and mitigates basin drift over long continuations.
We validate the approach on shape control of DLOs, with simulation and real-robot experiments on elastic strips under noise, modeling mismatch, and gravity.
Compared to gradient-free baselines (SPSA
(Spall,
2002
)
and CEM
(Botev
et al.
,
2013
)
),
Adjoint+RHC is 19вҖ“127
Г—
\times
faster and achieves 1вҖ“6 orders of magnitude lower best loss across all elastic strip manipulation tasks (Table
1
).
This paper makes the following contributions:
(i) an adjoint formulation that computes trajectory-dependent gradients by implicit differentiation of equilibrium conditions, enabling long-horizon optimization through sequences of equilibrium solves without unrolling inner Newton/CG iterations;
(ii) a receding-horizon continuation (RHC) scheme that repeatedly re-anchors optimization to realized equilibria and mitigates unintended basin switches in multi-stable regimes;
(iii) simulation and real-robot demonstrations on DLOs, including transfer under parameter mismatch and gravity.

2
Related works
Optimization and learning for long-horizon, path-dependent control.
Long-horizon control and sequential decision-making are central themes in machine learning, where performance is often limited by delayed credit assignment and partial observability, and, in model-based settings, by compounding model error.
A common response is to cast history dependence as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) and augment policies and value functions with memory, e.g., recurrent reinforcement learning (RL)
(Hausknecht and Stone,
2015
)
, or to learn latent state-space models that support planning via imagined rollouts
(Hafner
et al.
,
2019b
,
a
)
.
Another dominant paradigm is model-based RL with receding-horizon planning: actions are optimized over a short horizon and repeatedly replanned using model predictive control (MPC) to reduce error accumulation and improve robustness under regime changes
(Chua
et al.
,
2018
)
.
Complementary to these approaches, black-box and sampling-based optimizers remain strong baselines for long-horizon problems because they avoid differentiating through complex transitions and can explore multi-modal outcomes.
Representative examples include simultaneous perturbation stochastic approximation (SPSA)
(Spall,
2002
)
, the cross-entropy method (CEM)
(Botev
et al.
,
2013
)
, and parameter-space search methods such as evolution strategies (ES)
(Salimans
et al.
,
2017
)
and augmented random search (ARS)
(Mania
et al.
,
2018
)
. Recent work also makes CEM differentiable (DCEM)
(Amos and Yarats,
2020
)
.
However, the rollout cost of these methods typically grows quickly with horizon length and action dimension, which becomes especially acute when each step involves an expensive implicit equilibrium computation
(Pinneri
et al.
,
2021
)
.
Our method retains receding-horizon replanning but computes physics-informed, trajectory-level sensitivities through implicit transitions along the realized branch, enabling efficient gradient-based optimization.
Memory-efficient, trajectory-based gradients for long horizons.
Backpropagation through time (BPTT) provides a direct way to obtain long-horizon, trajectory-dependent gradients by propagating sensitivities across timesteps
(Williams and Peng,
1990
)
, but naively storing intermediate states makes memory scale with horizon length, motivating checkpointing and recomputation strategies
(Gruslys
et al.
,
2016
)
.
In differentiable physics and control, this challenge becomes more pronounced when each transition is an implicit update solved via inner iterations (e.g., Newton or conjugate gradient), since differentiating through solver iterations is memory-intensive and can be numerically brittle near instabilities
(Amos and Kolter,
2017
; Suh
et al.
,
2022
)
.
Inspired by adjoint sensitivity methods
(Chen
et al.
,
2018
)
, a complementary line of work avoids unrolling by differentiating through implicit functions via optimality or fixed-point conditions, including deep equilibrium models
(Bai
et al.
,
2019
)
and differentiable implicit layers
(Look
et al.
,
2020
)
.
Related ideas also appear in differentiable control, such as differentiable MPC via KKT conditions
(Amos
et al.
,
2018
)
and Pontryagin differentiable programming (PDP)
(Jin
et al.
,
2020
)
.
In contrast to DEQ/implicit layers that differentiate a single fixed point, we address long-horizon optimization through
sequences
of equilibria where multi-stability makes the realized trajectory branch-dependent.
In contrast to differentiable MPC/PDP, our setting requires sensitivities through an implicit equilibrium solve at every step rather than explicit dynamics or a single KKT system.
Compared to BPTT through solver iterations, we avoid unrolling inner solves and use a proxy-adjoint recursion; we further adopt a frozen-tangent proxy to avoid higher-order derivatives along long horizons.
Differentiable models for learning and control.
Differentiable modeling and simulation have become core primitives for gradient-based learning, planning, and inverse problems, and recent ML toolchains such as DiffTaichi
(Hu
et al.
,
2019a
)
and Brax
(Freeman
et al.
,
2021
)
make optimizing through physics practical at scale.
Differentiable simulators have been extended from rigid bodies to deformables and soft bodies, including ChainQueen
(Hu
et al.
,
2019b
)
, DiffPD
(Du
et al.
,
2021
)
, and differentiable soft multi-body simulation
(Qiao
et al.
,
2021
)
.
For flexible structures, discrete differential geometry (DDG) discretizations provide geometry-preserving, energy-based models with analytic derivatives
(Bergou
et al.
,
2008
; Grinspun
et al.
,
2003
; Huang
et al.
,
2025
)
.
Benchmarks further clarify the difficulty of long-horizon deformable manipulation, e.g., SoftGym
(Lin
et al.
,
2021
)
and PlasticineLab
(Huang
et al.
,
2021
)
.
Learned differentiable simulators can offer fast predictors, but near multistability and bifurcation boundaries deterministic models can blur branch selection without explicit multimodality (e.g., mixture formulations)
(Sanchez-Gonzalez
et al.
,
2020
; Pfaff
et al.
,
2021
; Bishop,
1994
)
.
Recent work also integrates analytic gradients from differentiable multiphysics simulations into RL updates (e.g., SAPO)
(Xing
et al.
,
2025
)
.
Our work complements these efforts by focusing on the gradient pathway itself: memory-efficient, trajectory-level sensitivities through sequences of implicit equilibrium solves so optimization follows the realized branch.

4
Results
We evaluate our method on elastic-strip manipulation tasks (Figure
1
A).
The strip undergoes large deformations and admits multi-stable equilibria, inducing strong path dependence: small continuation errors can accumulate and trigger undesired switches between equilibrium branches.
We consider three tasks that progressively stress these effects: (i) point-to-point manipulation (terminal objective),
(ii) path following of a designated strip point (trajectory objective), and (iii) shape formation in multi-stable regimes (branch selection).
All methods share the same forward rollout, which computes strip configurations by solving implicit equilibria to convergence at each continuation step.
We compare our full method (Adjoint+RHC) against SPSA and CEM, and ablate our approach by removing RHC (Adjoint-only).
Each method is run for the same number of parameter-update steps
T
T
(optimizer updates on
Оҳ
\Theta
).
An objective evaluation corresponds to one full rollout that computes the task loss from implicit equilibrium solves.
Because SPSA uses two rollouts per update (two-sided perturbations) and CEM uses
P
P
rollouts per update (population evaluation), these gradient-free methods consume multiple objective evaluations per update.
Adjoint-only and Adjoint+RHC use one rollout per update and additionally solve tangent/adjoint linear systems to obtain gradients, avoiding backpropagation through the Newton/CG iterations of the equilibrium solver.
We therefore report both best achieved loss and total wall-clock time, reflecting rollout cost and gradient overhead.
Finally, we validate the approach in real-world robotic experiments.

Convergence and robustness.
Figure
4
plots the log best-so-far objective value versus optimizer updates (defined as iterations) for all methods and tasks.
TaskВ 1 (node targeting) is the simplest setting: all methods reduce the loss early, but SPSA and CEM plateau mid-training
and fail to reach the tolerance within the budget on some cases. Both adjoint-based variants continue improving and
reach lower final loss, with Adjoint-only converging fastest in this short-horizon regime where proxy-adjoint
gradients remain accurate.
TaskВ 2 (trajectory tracking) is substantially harder because the objective depends on intermediate equilibria along the
realized branch.
SPSA and CEM again stall and fail under the fixed update budget. Adjoint-only succeeds on smooth
references but fails on non-smooth targets (e.g., square-wave and triangular trajectories), where long-horizon accumulation makes optimization brittle. In contrast, Adjoint+RHC achieves consistent convergence across all trajectory
types, indicating that segment-wise re-anchoring to realized equilibria is critical for stable trajectory-level control.
TaskВ 3 (multistable shape formation) is the most challenging regime: multiple stable equilibria coexist under identical
boundary conditions, so small errors can induce undesired basin switches. SPSA and CEM stagnate early, and Adjoint-only,
despite a fast initial decrease, often become trapped in an incorrect branch and fail the curvature tolerance. Adjoint+RHC
consistently reaches the lowest curvature losses and reliably selects the specified equilibrium branch, demonstrating the
importance of combining adjoint-based gradients with RHC in multistable landscapes.

5
Discussion
Our results highlight two recurring difficulties in equilibrium-governed control: (i) obtaining usable gradients when states are defined only implicitly by a converged equilibrium solve, and (ii) maintaining branch consistency over long continuations in multi-stable landscapes. We address (i) by keeping the forward pass exact (each rollout state is a converged equilibrium) while computing proxy-adjoint gradients that avoid differentiating through NewtonвҖ“CG iterations. Despite the frozen-tangent approximation, these gradients are sufficiently informative to optimize terminal and trajectory objectives. We address (ii) with RHC, which localizes proxy/discretization error to short segments and repeatedly re-anchors optimization at realized equilibria. This wrapper is most beneficial in multi-stable regimes, where it reduces basin drift and improves reliability of branch selection.
Limitations include frozen-tangent bias (ignored

and higher-order terms), reduced reliability near bifurcations or under non-smooth physics, and limited lookahead in segment-wise updates, which may require stronger terminal objectives or shaping to solve long-horizon tasks. Equilibrium solves remain the dominant cost, motivating better warm-starting, preconditioning, and adaptive step sizing.
6
Conclusion
We presented proxy-adjoint neural continuation for control and learning in equilibrium-defined systems, combining exact equilibrium rollouts with efficient implicit sensitivities and a receding-horizon continuation (RHC) wrapper for robust long-horizon branch tracking. Empirically, Adjoint+RHC achieves large efficiency gains: it is 19вҖ“127
Г—
\times
faster than SPSA/CEM across TasksВ 1вҖ“3 while reaching 3вҖ“6 orders of magnitude lower best loss on the same fixed update budgets. Finally, we demonstrate qualitative sim-to-real transfer on a 6-DoF robotic setup under gravity and experimentally identified material parameters (Figure
5
), indicating that proxy-adjoint continuation is a practical tool for real manipulation of compliant, multi-stable structures.

preview_text:
Many physical AI tasks are governed by implicit equilibrium: an agent actuates a subset of degrees of freedom (boundary DoFs), while the remaining free DoFs settle by minimizing a total potential energy. Even seemingly basic tasks such as bending a deformable linear object (DLO) to a target shape can exhibit strongly nonlinear behavior due to multi-stability: the same boundary conditions may yield multiple equilibrium shapes depending on the actuation trajectory. However, learning and control in such systems is brittle because the actuation-to-configuration map is defined only implicitly, and naive backpropagation through iterative equilibrium solvers is memory- and compute-intensive. We propose Neural Control, a boundary-control framework that computes trajectory-dependent, memory-efficient proxy gradients by differentiating equilibrium conditions via an adjoint formulation, avoiding unrolling of solver iterations. To improve robustness over long horizons, we integrate these sensitivities into a receding-horizon MPC scheme that repeatedly re-anchors optimization to realized equilibria and mitigates basin-switching in multi-stable regimes. We evaluate Neural Control in simulation and on physical robots manipulating DLOs, and show improved performance over gradient-free baselines such as SPSA and CEM.

Neural Control: Adjoint Learning Through Equilibrium Constraints
